entry.title: Recommendation for configuration role based on standard template SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts, 

We are creating the business roles in an S/4HANA Public Cloud system. We were planning to create a role for access to configure in implementation activities as a copy of the SAP standard business role template SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT to grant access to all the configuration points. 

The problem we are facing is that the business role template SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT in our system has almost 300 catalogs, and we get this message when working with the business role:

And then checking the Best Practices for Business Roles | SAP Help Portal in fact SAP is recommending "Not more than 100 business catalogs should be assigned to a business role".

My questions:

  1. Is there any SAP recommendation on how to handle business roles based on the business role template SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT?
  2. Is there any SAP recommendation on how to set up the security to grant permissions to update the configuration in an S/4HANA Public Cloud system? 

Thanks.

entry.title: Re: Integrate SAP S/4HANA on-premises with ActiveMQ on-pr...
entry.content:
Almost.

If you'd be wanting to use APIs of S/4HANA on-premise then you can use EIC to locally connect to them and consume/proxy the data.

If you'd be wanting to use Events of S/4HANA on-premise then you need an Event Broker. Two event-enabled systems do not connect to each other, they connect to a shared broker. You could look at Advanced Event Mesh which can have brokers locally installed in your K8S. Depending on what you want to do with the events, there might be no need for EIC (event consumer) if you already have another AMQP consumer as intended target.

The JMS adapter inside EIC is for internal async usage only (internal event broker to facilitate handover between iflows) and can not be used to connect to external event brokers (this is a roadmap item) and certainly not to event producers directly (non-brokers).

The AMQP adapter inside EIC can be used to connect to external AMQP-capable event brokers, such as Advanced Event Mesh.

With Basic Authentication, you may not be able to use clientId and clientSecret.

entry.title: CALM: How is the Upgrade Roadmap used in parallel with an Implementation Roadmap?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Upgrade Roadmap states:

"The roadmap can be used as a standalone mini project for a customer who is already live with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, without ongoing projects. It can also be used in parallel, or have tasks added to an ongoing new implementation or continuous improvement project."

My understanding is therefore that SAP explicitly supports using upgrade activities during an ongoing implementation project.

However, in SAP Cloud ALM, a project appears to be assigned to a single SAP Activate roadmap.

My question is:

How is SAP's recommendation implemented in SAP Cloud ALM when upgrade activities need to be managed in parallel with an ongoing implementation?

For example:

  • Do you create a second SAP Cloud ALM project based on the Upgrade Roadmap?
  • Do you manually add the upgrade tasks to the implementation project?
  • Is there any way to consume both roadmaps within the same project?

I would appreciate any guidance from SAP or implementation partners who have managed Public Cloud projects across mandatory SAP release upgrades.

Thank you.

entry.title: Issue with Dynpro Screen Download/Upload Between Systems After Namespace Change
entry.content:
We migrated a custom SAP development from System A to System B and changed the namespace during the process.

Example:
/ABC/PROGRAM → YXYZ_PROGRAM

Most objects work fine after renaming. However, when we download a Dynpro/screen from System A and upload it into System B, we get a “screen overflow” error. Even after updating all obvious references to the new namespace, the screen still doesn’t work correctly.


Has anyone encountered screen overflow or Dynpro upload issues after a namespace change? Are there hidden dependencies in screens that don’t get updated automatically during download/upload?
We migrated a custom SAP development from System A to System B and changed the namespace during the process.

Example:
/ABC/PROGRAM → YXYZ_PROGRAM

Most objects work fine after renaming. However, when we download a Dynpro/screen from System A and upload it into System B, we get a “screen overflow” error. Even after updating all obvious references to the new namespace, the screen still doesn’t work correctly.

Has anyone encountered screen overflow or Dynpro upload issues after a namespace change? Are there hidden dependencies in screens that don’t get updated automatically during download/upload?

entry.title: Re: Previous period posting
entry.content: Check Posting period is open or closed. Backdating is generally not recommended as it impacts stock consistency and reporting. If period is open MIGO  go to general data tab locate the posting date field enter the desired past date.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management 2605
entry.content:
SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management (SAP ICSM) is an advanced solution for clinical trials to facilitate study management, demand forecasting, manufacturing, packaging, labeling, and shipments of clinical trial materials.   

Today, we are excited to share the highlights of Release 2605 for SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management. This release brings significant enhancements across planning, study management, compliance, user experience, and manufacturing, all designed to help clinical supply teams work faster, smarter, and with greater confidence.

Read on for the key highlights across all areas.

 

Program Management

Compare Multiple Placeholder Study Scenarios

Program managers can now compare up to three scenarios of a placeholder study side-by-side directly in the Manage Programs app. The new compare menu on the forecast toolbar enables parallel analysis of demand forecasting results and planning figures, supporting faster, evidence-based decisions.

Enhanced Combination Therapy Modeling

Users can now choose to define treatment duration, visit frequency, and start offset at the material level within placeholder study scenarios. This makes it possible to accurately model complex combination therapy regimens (including staggered start/end times and varying visit schedules) directly during early program planning, long before detailed supply forecasting begins.

 

Study Management and Demand Forecasting

Smarter Study Search:

The Manage Studies app now lets you filter studies by specific study settings, such as study category, kit type specific depots, automatic side seeding, and demand recalculation schedule. Combined with variant management and the adapt filters option, you can save and reuse your preferred filter combinations. Finding the right study now takes seconds instead of minutes.

 

No More Gaps in Technical IDs:

When study entities such as kit types, blinding groups, or treatment groups are deleted, the system now automatically reuses the freed-up technical IDs, starting with the lowest available number. This eliminates unexplained numbering gaps and makes audit trails and compliance documentation significantly cleaner.

Copy Kit Type Design Including Component Identifiers

When reusing an existing kit type design (KTD) for a new study, users can now choose whether to copy component identifiers (CIDs) along with it, and if so, whether to include the existing responses or copy the structure only. If you choose not to carry over the original CIDs, the system still ensures that any identifiers marked as mandatory or default-visible in your configuration are added automatically to the new design. This eliminates a common source of manual errors and missed identifiers during study setup, saving time and giving teams greater confidence when building on existing kit designs.

 

Visualize Material Code Status in Study Master

Knowing the current status of a material (e.g. whether it is active for use, blocked for procurement, being phased out, or marked as obsolete) is essential for compliant and informed supply planning. Release 2605 introduces the cross-plant material status as a new column in the kit type table at both label group and site group levels within the Manage Studies app, and also makes it available in the Material Overview section. The status is fetched in real time from the SAP S/4HANA material master (MARA) and can additionally be included as a selectable value in approval item templates. This gives clinical and supply chain teams immediate visibility into material lifecycle status, reducing the risk of compliance issues and supporting better material selection decisions throughout the study.

Enhanced Material Request Information in Workflow Inbox

When trial managers and workflow recipients handle manual material requests, having the right context is critical for fast and accurate decisions. In release 2605, the workflow inbox now displays a significantly richer set of details for each request, including study description, study status, therapeutic area, lead molecule, packaging site, temporary material code, repository description, and the active countries for the assigned label group. Contacts are presented in a sortable table view, and country lists open in a clean popover showing only active entries. This improvement applies to manual material requests and reduces back-and-forth communication by putting all relevant study, kit, and material parameters directly in front of the person handling the request.

Kit Type Comments

Each kit type can now carry its own free-text comment (up to 500 characters), visible directly in the app, in approval items, and tracked in the Change Log app. This improves transparency and communication during study setup, and any post-approval changes trigger deviation management automatically.

Dynamic Visit Recalculation Based on IRT Actuals

A new optional feature allows future patient visits to be automatically rescheduled whenever IRT (Interactive Response Technology system) actual visit dates are received. All subsequent visits are recalculated from the latest real visit, not the original plan. This is especially critical for make-to-order protocols such as radioligand therapy, where timing between visits directly affects supply preparation.

 

 

Change History

Comprehensive Audit-Ready Export

The View Change History app (formerly named Export Change History) has been significantly enhanced. You can now export the full system change history, including configuration changes, to well-formatted Excel files suitable for internal and external audit requirements. Multi-object export (up to 20 objects at once, each on its own tab) and attribute-level detail are now supported. A dedicated export authorization role ensures only designated users can perform exports.

 

Manufacturing & Packaging

Approval of Country Lists for Booklet Materials with 4-Eye Check

Additions and deletions to country lists for booklet materials now require approval, with full support for a configurable 4-eye principle check. The Manage Country Lists for Booklets app displays approval status at both country and list level, all status changes are logged, and a warning is shown in the Clinical Supply Orders app if unapproved country list changes exist for a label group. When a user uploads a spreadsheet, the system adds only new countries rather than replacing the entire existing list. This ensures that booklet country assignments always go through a controlled, auditable review process - reducing the risk of compliance gaps and incorrect label distribution to unauthorized markets.

 

Block Medication Kits for Destruction

The Withdraw Batches app now supports blocking medication kits, completely or partially, for the purpose of destruction. The stock type of the affected batch is set to Blocked, preventing these kits from being considered in MRP planning. Depending on configuration, the system either posts the goods movement directly or creates a delivery document for SAP EWM and 3PL locations. An automation report (/CTCO/WITHDRAW_BATCHES) is also available for scheduled execution. This gives supply and quality teams a fast, controlled, and fully traceable mechanism to remove expired or unusable kits from the supply chain without relying on manual workarounds.

 

Inner Label Printing During Shelf-Life Revision

In response to new EU regulatory requirements for expiry dates on inner labels, it is now possible to trigger inner label print orders during shelf-life revision, in addition to the already supported outer label printing. This is configured at the depot level, and the relevant BOM structure is used to determine the label components, printing supplier, and printing location. For clinical supply teams operating in regulated European markets, this eliminates a manual gap in the shelf-life revision process and ensures that updated expiry information reaches both the outer and inner packaging consistently and compliantly.

New PCN Changes App

The new Fiori app “PCN Changes” provides a full change log for Packaging Control Number (PCN) objects, capturing events from creation through batch changes, approvals, releases, and medication number assignments. It includes changes made both directly in the PCN and via the Clinical Supply Orders app, and can be filtered by study, protocol, responsible employee, PCN status, and more. By bringing all PCN-related change history into a single dedicated app, quality and supply teams can respond to audit requests faster and with greater confidence without having to piece together information from multiple sources.

 

Distribution

Reversal Process: Purchase Order – Inbound Delivery

Incorrect medication kit assignments during the Purchase Order – Inbound Delivery (PO-IBDL) process have historically required complex manual corrections. Release 2605 introduces a complete rollback capability for this process: when an inbound delivery is deleted, the system guides the user to also mark the related PO item as deleted and release a new one. The incorrectly assigned Medication numbers are automatically restored to status "Available", so they can be reused in a subsequent Purchase Order, eliminating the need to generate new numbers. Throughout the entire reversal, a full change log is maintained on the impacted Medication Kit, ensuring complete traceability for compliance and audit purposes.

 

Reversal Process: Initial Stock Upload

Similar rollback capabilities are now also available for the Initial Stock Upload process, covering both SAP Inventory Management (IM) and SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) locations. If medication kits were incorrectly assigned during an initial stock upload, the process can now be fully reversed: the goods movement is cancelled, the medication kit is reset to an undefined state, and the previously assigned medication numbers are returned to "Available" for reuse. As with the PO-IBDL reversal, the complete change history is preserved on the medication kit throughout, giving quality and supply teams the audit trail they need without any manual workaround.

Bucket Value Configuration Refinements

The Bucket Value Configuration has been substantially simplified and hardened in release 2605. A new SAP-delivered switch business configuration set provides a minimum viable example out of the box, covering 10 common use cases mapped to 7 bucket values, giving new implementations a solid starting point without needing to build everything from scratch. Validations have been added to restrict configuration entries to supported movement types only, preventing misuse in unsupported scenarios. Several previously configurable behaviors are now automatic: reversal postings restore the previous medication kit status without any configuration entry, and the "In Rework" (INRE) bucket value no longer needs to be maintained in the config table at all.
The configuration table itself has been renamed to Map Stock Buckets and Status to Goods Movement Type and Reason for clarity.

 

Summary

Release 2605 delivers meaningful improvements across the SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management solution, from smarter study and scenario management to tighter compliance controls, richer audit trails, and a more consistent user experience. Whether you are a trial manager, supply planner, or system administrator, this release has something that makes your day-to-day work more efficient and more reliable.

Please keep in mind that this blog only summarizes the release highlights. To explore all the details, refer to the official release documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

We look forward to hearing your feedback - feel free to leave a comment below or reach out to the product team directly.

entry.title: Don't Take It On Blind Faith
entry.content:
A free all day event is challenging to arrange in the latest iterations of the SAP Community, where once a Philadelphia region was rebranded as Mid-Atlantic. Naturally an easy place to get together is the Newtown Square campus, though downtown Philly packs a lot more culture in a smaller distance. I should map that.

Link back to the day's agenda and the primary thrust seems to be AI, yet the speakers addressed current and future software practices from different perspectives and focused on classic specific topics such as service parts management and sales planning. Rather than do a traditional chronological step-through of the day, I'll pick items that stood out to me, leaving the title reference till last.

Argh with the tiny print on slides. We're not looking over your shoulder at a 21-inch monitor, we're in the back of a room.

150kb JPEG for effect

I appreciated hearing terms like guardrails, skills, sentiment analysis (from a community audience member), trust, authentication, (human) quality gate. The "jail break" discussion was fruitful except it needed to continue over pints in a pub later (maybe it did but I ducked out to avoid late-afternoon thunderstorms plus Friday traffic).

What I didn't hear about was litigation, copyright, environmental impacts, and software/data quality assurance in general (despite specific real-world case studies), nor that much about budgets/spending. Would have been interesting to hear architectures that help "the business" like dev/test/prod, which admittedly I'm a decades or more out of synch with a corporate engine room. 

Agents arguing was a somewhat humorous anecdote, like the old Mad Magazine Spy Versus Spy. Which "authority" will prevail? Stay tuned; I think the takeaway was it's not a push button it has to be iterated. 

Cool moment was a power flicker when we were in the campus cafe. It seemed to only affect a few things, not getting dark, so my lunch coupon was waived/waved through. Thanks, sponsors.

The fine print at the top says "The Developer Behind This Project" and @RichHeilman 's T shirt says something like "I consume APIs".

 

I asked about quality assurance, referencing a special interest group ASUG had that generally dealt with scale up, but also fitness for purpose. These days, I don't know what's in that tent or under the umbrella, pick your simile, for making sure "the code" does what it's supposed to, and to quote another hopefully obscure Python reference: "and Nothing Else."

Testing is a "grey area", agreed, with part of the equation budgets, part human brain power, and part experience.

Another random Python quip, this time the code not the troupe:

# Writing tests

Instructions about writing tests for the processing framework
can be found in a [separate README file](../python/plugins/processing/tests/README.md):

Writing good tests is both an art and a science, not to mention the discipline to keep ahead of the curve of new "attack vectors" like an ongoing repository poisoning that was happening to Arch Linux the same day as the meet-up.

Rich had a demo of a self-written "app" which had either 3,000 or 30.000 lines of code, I wasn't sure, none of which Rich said he wrote (directly), involving a home music library, writ large.  I won't comment on the aspects of addiction mentioned, but point out that accepting some data feed or content on "blind faith" is problematic. Fortunately for the gauntlet of the live demo, there was not an embarrassing moment that Rich would have not expected based on him saying he'd never heard of them.

In this case, the font is legible, cropped to only part of the display (and maybe my phone has better eyesight than I do, ha). Except I can't show it to you under a nondisclosure claim.

Rich describes the building

"Rich reviews & iterates" is the important part, far right, in red. "Running" means executable, to me, not "published to the world."

End Notes

  • Hopefully, I'll get permission to share the slide content greyed out above.
  • Lunch was great, caught up with longtime colleagues.
  • Met @smarchesini in person after years of non-events. Might need to get to an NYC event, as the train is much different than driving the back roads around Newtown Square.
  • Also met @GoLaSalle @greg_not_so @johna69 of course: @StephanieMarley but not Thomas Jung this time. @josh_bentley with a omnipresence. @Deise was a new contact, and I somehow didn't catch the spelling until I looked back at the roster (not "Daisy" as I heard in my head).  Anyone else I apologize for not meeting!
  • So many new TLAs and other size acronyms.

entry.title: Re: SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation
entry.content:
Hi @ChingHong 

Thank you for your comment!

The Destination Certificates UI only exposes the public certificate - the private key is not available through the UI. As noted in the SAP Help documentation, the private key can only be obtained via the Destination Service REST API. Since generating a PFX requires both the certificate and the private key, the UI alone is not sufficient for this purpose.

If you check the UI generated .pem file, it also shows that only a public key is present, hence not usable for inbound client certificate based authentication. If the sender system does not have the private key, the mTLS handshake can not complete.

Hope this helps, and let me know if you have any further questions!

 

Best Regards,
Adam

entry.title: The agent in the SAP World
entry.content:
What is an agent in the SAP world, and how do you build one in Joule Studio

A written version of the talk I gave at SAP Inside Track Philadelphia on June 12, 2026.

I gave this talk at SIT Philadelphia last week. A few people asked for the slides, and the slides on their own don't say much without me standing next to them, so here is the written version. If you were in the room, this is the recap. If you weren't, this is the whole argument.

Quick note on why I bothered putting this together. I came 2nd at the global Hack2Build competition on Joule Studio, and that gave me a hands-on view of how SAP custom agents actually behave. What they're good at, where they break, how to design around it. That's the perspective I'm writing from. Not a product pitch.

One more thing before the technical part, and I mean it. The whole talk is about what AI can do, but the reason I drove to Philadelphia for SIT wasn't the slides. It was the people. SAP Inside Track events, the location-based community groups, the hallway conversations after a session, that's where the real work happens. You meet the person who already hit the bug you're about to hit. You find the partner you'll build with next year. You get the honest answer that never makes it into a blog post. An agent can summarize a document and draft an email, but it can't shake your hand, or vouch for you to a client. The relationships are the one thing none of this technology replaces. If you take agents seriously, take the community more seriously. Show up.

The short version: building an agent is fast now. Making one that's safe to run inside an SAP landscape is not. The gap between those two things is the whole job.

Why this is harder than it looks

 

Anyone can stand up an agent in an afternoon. The problem starts the moment you point it at real SAP data.

A generic agent built outside SAP has no idea what an authorization role is. It doesn't know what a sales order header is, or a chart of accounts. You can rebuild that context, but it costs weeks per use case, and you're doing it again for the next agent.

Then there's the running side. Raw MCP servers expose APIs with no policy, no rate limiting, no audit trail. One AI prompt can fan out into thousands of API calls, and transactional APIs were never built for trial and error. An agent stuck in a loop can take down your ERP. By the time security catches up, the thing is already in production.

This isn't theoretical. DSAG's 2026 Investment Survey found only 3% of SAP customers run AI in production, even though 77% of AI-active enterprises use AI tools day to day. The pilots work. They just don't ship.

So the real question isn't "can I build an agent." It's "can I build one that's grounded in my business, allowed to talk to my systems, and governed when it does."

Why you can't just wire up any agent

 

SAP put out an API policy that answers part of this for you, and it's worth understanding before you architect anything.

The policy is unified API governance across SAP cloud and on-prem, including S/4HANA Private Cloud and RISE. It standardizes the rate limits and throttling that products like SuccessFactors, Ariba, and LeanIX already use. It does not change your license grants or entitlements. The APIs you have stay available exactly as documented.

What it does block is ungoverned agents hitting transactional APIs directly. Three reasons, and all three are fair:

  • System stability. One prompt, thousands of calls. Transactional APIs can't absorb that.
  • Data security. An ungoverned agent has no verifiable identity and can reach data outside its scope.
  • Data integrity. An agent without business context will write wrong data back, confidently.

SAP isn't saying don't build agents. It's saying come in through a governed door. The endorsed doors are the MCP Gateway on Integration Suite, the Agent2Agent protocol through Joule and the Agent Gateway, SAP Business Data Cloud with Delta Sharing for bulk data, and the reference architectures on the SAP Architecture Center.

If you read nothing else from this section, read these two. They're the main idea for where SAP is taking this, and the policy above only makes sense once you've seen them:

  • SAP's AI Golden Path is the practical starting point. It walks you through the AI technology stack, helps you decide between ML, LLM, and agentic approaches, and covers design, deliver, run, evaluate, and extend. If you're architecting an AI application on SAP, start here.
  • SAP AI-native North Star architecture is the target vision. It's not a roadmap or a product commitment. It's the direction: data and AI as one intelligent core, a deterministic path for the rule-based work compliance depends on, and a probabilistic path for reasoning, bound together by context, guardrails, and observability. The Golden Path is how you build today. The North Star is what you're building toward.

That pairing is the whole bet. One path stays reliable and rigid, the other adds reasoning that's powerful but, without context and control, often confidently wrong. The architecture exists to make the two work together.

The recommendation

 

Here's where I landed, and it's the same conclusion SAP's POV series lands on.

Build inside SAP, not next to it. Build custom agents in Joule Studio so they inherit Joule's security model, business semantics, and access to S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, and Ariba from day one. No parallel permission model to maintain. The agent sees what the user is authorized to see, and nothing else.

Connect with SAP-native patterns. Ground agents in business data through HANA Cloud, the Knowledge Graph, and Business Data Cloud instead of copying everything into a separate AI stack. Use MCP for tool access and A2A so Joule agents, code-based agents, and partner agents can collaborate.

Compose, don't lock in. Joule Studio speaks MCP and A2A natively. You orchestrate across frameworks rather than betting the house on one.

The reason this works is simple. SAP's AI-native model treats data, context, and process as one fabric. If you run the agent where the meaning already lives, you stop re-creating context for free.

Four ways to build, and what each one costs you

 

This is the slide I spent the most time on, because the choice is real and there's no single right answer.

Joule Studio. Native integration with Joule and S/4HANA context, low-code authoring, governed by SAP identity and lifecycle. Fastest time to value. The trade-off is you're limited to SAP-supported models and patterns, with less room for complex custom logic.

A2A. Open protocol, interoperable across vendors, reuse agents from any framework, decoupled architecture that's easier to evolve. The standard is still maturing, cross-agent governance is harder, and you need an orchestration and discovery layer.

Core AI (Generative AI Hub). Full control over models, prompts, and tools, with access to the Generative AI Hub models. This is for the highly custom, domain-specific cases. It's also the heaviest build. Pro-code required, you own the evals and guardrails and lifecycle, and it's the longest path to production.

Integration Suite. Exposes SAP and non-SAP systems as MCP tools, reuses your existing iFlows and connectors, centralizes security and monitoring. The cost is an extra integration hop with latency to manage, and it needs Integration Suite licensing.

Most teams don't pick one. They start with Joule Studio for a use case, put Integration Suite in front of the tool calls, and add A2A once they have a second agent. More on that order at the end.

Inside Joule Studio

This is where I dropped the personal slides and went to the SAP official slides. 

 

 

 

If your requirement isn't covered by SAP's 40+ out-of-the-box Joule agents, the recommended path is Joule Studio's Agent Builder, generally available since Q1 2026. It's no-code and low-code with vibe coding built in. You describe what you want in plain English and the Joule copilot suggests an agent definition, the tools, and the configuration. You can bring the main LLM providers, and call external systems through MCP servers. Agents stay inside SAP's security landscape and show up across every app the user already touches.

 

 

 

 

This is the layout that made it click for the room. Joule Studio lives inside SAP Build. Inside the studio you have Actions (SAP and non-SAP APIs), Automation (processes, task automations, decisions), Tools (human-in-the-loop, documents, calculator, MCP servers, other agents), and AI models (GPT, Gemini, Mistral, Anthropic). It all runs on a managed Agent Runtime with security, governance, and monitoring underneath. Off to the side sit Joule skills and Joule agents, with AI Core and document grounding in the AI Foundation.

 

 

This is the official Architecture Center solution diagram for the same picture, in case you want the BTP-accurate version with subaccounts, destinations, and Cloud Identity Services trust (probably the most important part). 

Skills vs agents, which is the part most people get wrong

If you take one thing from the talk, take this. Joule Studio has two object types, and they are not the same thing.

 

You build your own Joule agents, and you extend Joule with custom Joule skills. Knowing which one a given problem needs is most of the design work.

 

 

An agent reasons. It plans, it's autonomous, it can talk to other agents, and it uses LLMs and tools to get somewhere. The path is unknown when it starts ( it's not deterministic ). Give it a problem, it figures out the route.

 

 

The four things agents actually do: plan multiple steps toward a goal, reflect and self-correct when they hit a wall, use tools to gather data and make changes, and collaborate with other agents and humans. That's the whole behavior model.

 

 

A skill is the opposite. It's deterministic and rule-based (THIS IS IMPORTANT!). The path is known. It's orchestrated through Joule, triggered by intent and context, and it runs a predefined flow. Retrieve supply chain risks, get weather conditions, raise a transport request, create a ServiceNow ticket. One job, done the same way every time.

 

 

Skills give you deterministic execution with fixed inputs and outputs, natural language understanding so phrasing doesn't matter, context awareness so they only fire when relevant, and the ability to combine inside one Joule instance. Reliable and boring, in the good way.

 

 

The cleanest way I found to hold the two in your head:

  • A skill says: tell me exactly what to do, and I'll do it. Deterministic, rule-based, atomic, known inputs and outputs. Exposed through Joule.
  • An agent says: tell me the problem, and I'll figure out the best way to solve it. Adaptive, multi-step. Exposed through Joule or external apps.

When you scope a project, the first question isn't "agent or skill." It's "is the path known." If it is, you want a skill, and reaching for an agent just adds cost and nondeterminism. If it isn't, that's when an agent earns its keep.

The demo: our early payment assistant

This is where I stopped talking about the theory and showed something real. The agent we built is an early payment assistant. The job it does is simple to describe and annoying to do by hand: given a stack of open invoices, decide which ones to pay first to capture the best early-payment discount from the vendor.

I can't show you the live system in a blog post, so I won't try to fake it with screenshots. What I walked the room through was the structure of the agent itself. How it's scoped, how it's grounded, how it reads the invoice data, and how it reasons about the discount-versus-cash trade-off. The point of showing the structure rather than the magic is this: once the agent exists with proper access to the SAP environment, the team can take it from there and build their own agents against the same foundation.

Now the honest part. The demo did not go well. 😅 I forgot my password, which is a great look in front of a room. We were tight on time. And I hadn't prepared enough data, so the agent had thin ground to stand on. 🙈 Shame on me. If you're presenting a live agent, prepare more sample data than you think you need and log in before you walk on stage. Lesson learned in public.

Even with all that, I walked away happy with it. The idea landed, people got the difference between a skill and an agent, and the best part was a lot of questions during the presentation. Which, going back to the top of this post, is the part that actually mattered.

Sources and further reading

The source POV series (SAP Community, Feb 2026)

  • POV 1: Create Custom Agents in SAP
  • POV 2: Agent-to-Agent Collaboration with SAP and non-SAP Agents
  • POV 3: Using Integration Suite as Your Governed MCP Server Platform
  • POV 4: Putting it all Together Across Your Enterprise

Joule Studio

  • Agent Builder in Joule Studio is now generally available: Build your own Joule agents today! (SAP Community)
  • Announcing New Joule Studio for Enterprise Scale Agentic Development (news.sap.com, May 2026)

Architecture and how-to

  • SAP's AI Golden Path (SAP Architecture Center)
  • AI-native North Star architecture (SAP Architecture Center)
  • SAP's AI-Native North Star Architecture (news.sap.com, June 2026)
  • Build AI Agents on SAP BTP (SAP Architecture Center)

Standards and protocols

  • Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol specification, the open spec backed by SAP and Google Cloud
  • Announcing the Agent2Agent Protocol (A2A) (Google Developers Blog)
  • Model Context Protocol

The benchmark

  • DSAG Investment Survey 2026, the source for the 3% in production / 77% on non-SAP tooling figures. Analyst write-up: DSAG: SAP customer spending selective, AI drags (Constellation Research)

entry.title: [Defense & Security] SAP Learning Curriculum for Defense & Security Industry
entry.content:
SAP Learning Curriculum for Defense & Security Industry

Learning SAP for the Defense & Security industry requires a solid understanding of industry‑specific processes, terminology, and operational context. To better support learners, SAP has structured and refreshed the Defense & Security training portfolio, offering a clear learning path from introductory topics to advanced topics.

This blog gives you an overview of the current IDS course lineup, explains how the courses fit together, and shows how the Industries Learning Group can support your learning journey.

The Defense & Security learning curriculum at a glance

SAP Defense & Security learning follows a progressive structure, enabling learners to build knowledge step by step:

  • IDS010 – Introduction and industry overview
  • IDS10 – Fundamental Defense & Security business processes
  • IDS20 – Organizational flexibility and advanced processes
  • IDS30 – Logistics and maintenance in Defense & Security
  • EWMEX1 – Explosives Management solution understanding
  • EWMEX2 – Basic configuration concepts for Explosives Management

Each course builds on the previous one, supporting learners as they deepen their understanding.

IDS010 – Introducing SAP Defense & Security Industry

IDS010 is the recommended starting point for anyone new to the Defense & Security industry solution.

You’ll gain:

  • Industry context and terminology
  • A high‑level understanding of Defense & Security challenges
  • An overview of how SAP supports defense organizations

Self‑paced learning (learning.sap.com): https://learning.sap.com/courses/introducing-the-defense-security-industry

Ideal for:

  • Newcomers to Defense & Security
  • Business users
  • Learners seeking industry context before deeper courses

IDS10 – Exploring Fundamentals of SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

IDS10 builds on the introduction and provides a structured overview of:

  • Core Defense & Security business processes
  • End‑to‑end conceptual scenarios
  • How SAP supports these processes at a high level

Instructor‑led / Virtual classroom: IDS10 - Introducing SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

Self‑paced learning (learning.sap.com): Exploring Fundamentals of SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

Ideal for:

  • Consultants
  • Functional roles
  • Learners preparing for project work

IDS20 – Exploring Organizational Flexibility in SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

IDS20 moves into advanced Defense & Security concepts, including:

  • Organizational flexibility
  • Capability‑based planning
  • Integration of personnel, finance, and logistics

Instructor‑led / Virtual classroom: IDS20 – Exploring Organizational Flexibility in SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

Self‑paced learning (learning.sap.com): Exploring Organizational Flexibility in SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

Ideal for:

  • Experienced consultants
  • Solution architects (from a learning perspective)
  • Learners expanding beyond foundational knowledge

IDS30 – Exploring Logistics and Maintenance in SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

IDS30 completes the learning path by focusing on:

  • Defense‑specific logistics processes
  • Maintenance integration
  • Operational readiness

Instructor‑led / Virtual classroom: IDS30 – Exploring Logistics and Maintenance in SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

Self‑paced learning (learning.sap.com): Exploring Logistics and Maintenance in SAP S/4HANA Defense & Security

Ideal for:

  • Learners working with logistics and maintenance topics
  • Practitioners seeking broader Defense & Security process understanding

Explosives Management with SAP EWM (EWMEX1 & EWMEX2)

For learners working in Defense & Security logistics environments, Explosives Management is a critical topic that goes beyond standard warehouse processes.

SAP provides dedicated learning content for Explosives Management based on SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). These courses complement the IDS learning path and are especially relevant for learners involved in ammunition, weapons, or other explosive materials scenarios.

EWMEX1 – Exploring Explosives Management in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

EWMEX1 focuses on understanding the Explosives Management solution in detail, including:

  • The reasons and regulatory background for Explosives Management
  • Key concepts and solution capabilities in SAP EWM
  • Safe storage, handling, and compliance mechanisms for explosive goods

Self‑paced learning (SAP Learning): Exploring Explosives Management in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

Ideal for:

  • Learners who completed IDS10 / IDS20 / IDS30
  • Defense & Security logistics learners
  • Anyone needing a conceptual understanding of Explosives Management

EWMEX2 – Exploring Basic Configuration of Explosives Management in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

EWMEX2 builds on the conceptual understanding and introduces the basic configuration concepts, including:

  • Item Unique Identification
  • Flexible bins and storage concepts
  • Planograph usage
  • Compliance checks for explosives stock management

Self‑paced learning (SAP Learning): Exploring Basic Configuration of Explosives Management in SAP Extended Warehouse Management

Ideal for:

  • Learners preparing for projects involving explosives logistics
  • Those who want to understand configuration concepts from a learning perspective
  • Learners transitioning toward logistics‑focused Defense & Security roles

What about older Defense & Security courses?

Some older Defense & Security training courses (e.g. DFPS66, DFPS67) have been retired as SAP solutions and industry enablement evolved.

If you have learned with older courses:

  • Use them as background knowledge
  • Align your learning with the current IDS010 → IDS30 path
  • Refresh your skills using updated SAP Learning and Training content

Staying aligned with the current course portfolio ensures your learning remains relevant.

Learn together in the Industries Learning Group

Learning does not stop with a single course. Many learners ask:

  • Which IDS course should I take next?
  • How do I combine industry learning with broader SAP topics?
  • How do I prepare myself before joining a project?

The Industries Learning Group is designed exactly for these questions.

In the group, you can:

  • Exchange with other Defense & Security learners
  • Ask learning‑oriented questions
  • Get guidance on learning paths and next steps

All Defense & Security posts are marked with:

  • [Defense & Security] in the title
  • Industry‑specific tags for easy filtering

For deep technical or implementation discussions, we will guide you to the appropriate expert communities.

Join the group and continue your learning journey

If you are:

  • New to SAP Defense & Security
  • Transitioning from older courses
  • Planning your next learning steps

Join the Industries Learning Group
Follow Defense & Security learning posts
Learn and exchange with other learners

Let’s build Defense & Security knowledge—step by step, together.

entry.title: Re: Authorization of Supplier Invoice Operation
entry.content:
Dear @s0016807163,

Recommended setup:

1. Purchaser role create / save / park only

Create a custom role, for example Z_BR_SUPPLIER_INVOICE_ENTRY by copying SAP_BR_AP_ACCOUNTANT then remove the posting-related supplier invoice catalogs:

  • SAP_MM_BC_INV_PROCESS_MC
  • SAP_MM_BC_INV_PROCESS_PC
  • SAP_MM_BC_INV_SCHEDULE_PC

SAP KBA 2834732 explicitly confirms this approach. After these catalogs are removed the user has no authorization to post supplier invoices and can only save/park invoices

More details below:

SAP KBA 2834732 - Restrict the authorization of users to post Supplier Invoices

2. Finance role posting allowed

Keep a separate Finance role, for example Z_BR_SUPPLIER_INVOICE_POSTING, based on SAP_BR_AP_ACCOUNTANT and keep the required supplier invoice processing catalogs, especially:

  • SAP_MM_BC_INV_PROCESS_MC
  • SAP_MM_BC_INV_PROCESS_PC

SAP KBA 3750266 confirms that these two catalogs provide authorization to post and reverse supplier invoices in the Supplier Invoices List app

More details below:

SAP KBA 3750266 - Reverse Button Missing For Supplier Invoice Documents

IMPORTANT: This requirement is mainly controlled by business catalogs, not by company-code-style restrictions. In Public Cloud, business roles contain business catalogs and the catalogs provide access to apps and actions. Restrictions then refine the data scope for example company code, but they are not the right mechanism to split create invoice from post invoice when the posting action comes from the assigned catalog

More details below:

SAP Learning Maintaining Business Roles in SAP S/4HANA, Public Cloud

Summary:

Create a reduced purchaser role without the supplier invoice posting catalogs and keep those catalogs only in the Finance role. Do not rely only on restrictions for this requirement and verify all other roles assigned to the purchaser because another role can still grant the missing posting authorization

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Request for Updated Crystal Reports Runtime (.NET 64-bit) – libpng Vulnerability (CVE-2026-22801)
entry.content:
Dear SAP Team,

We would like to raise a concern regarding the SAP Crystal Reports Runtime Engine for .NET Framework (64-bit), specifically SP38 and SP40.

Issue Summary
We have identified that the runtime package currently includes a version of the libpng library (version 1.6.37, or generally below 1.6.54), which is affected by a known security vulnerability (CVE-2026-22801).

Impact

  • As part of our organization’s security and compliance policies, vulnerable libraries are automatically removed or blocked during the patching process.
  • During this process, the libpng DLL associated with Crystal Reports is being removed, as it does not meet the required security version threshold.
  • This is causing functional impact on applications (e.g., failure in Excel report generation) that depend on the Crystal Reports runtime.

Request
We would like to request the following from SAP:

  • Please provide an updated version of the SAP Crystal Reports Runtime (SP38/SP40 or later) that includes a patched and compliant version of libpng (preferably 1.6.58 or above).
  • Alternatively, please advise if there is an official patch, fix pack, or workaround available to address this vulnerability without impacting functionality.
  • Any guidance on recommended configuration or mitigation approaches to ensure both security compliance and runtime stability would be appreciated.

Additional Context
This issue is impacting our production environment due to enforced vulnerability remediation controls, and timely resolution is critical to maintain reporting functionality.

We appreciate your support and guidance on this matter.

Kind regards,
Yot Yoon

entry.title: Billing Document Cancelled and IRN Not Generated – APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS BAdI Not Triggered for
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We have a business requirement in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud where customer emails should not be sent when a Billing Document is cancelled and the IRN is not generated.

To achieve this requirement, we implemented the Custom Logic BAdI APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS for application object BILLING_DOCUMENT, intending to control the email recipients dynamically based on the billing document status and IRN generation status.

Implementation Details:

  • Business Context: APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS

  • Application Object Type: BILLING_DOCUMENT

  • Implementation Status: Published and Active

  • Output Channel: Email

Issue:
The BAdI implementation is never triggered at runtime. We added executable statements and breakpoints at the beginning of the implementation, but the debugger never stops, indicating that the BAdI is not being called.

Could you please confirm:

  1. Is APOC_ADD_EMAIL_RECIPIENTS supported for BILLING_DOCUMENT email outputs in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?

  2. Can this BAdI be used to control customer email recipients based on Billing Document cancellation and IRN generation status?

  3. Are there any additional prerequisites, configuration steps, or known limitations for this BAdI to be triggered for Billing Document email outputs?

Any guidance would be highly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Payroll Posting to FI Error (3G103) After Processing Class 20 Correction on Wage Type 1419
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I am facing an issue with Payroll Posting to FI in SAP HCM Payroll (International Payroll - MOLGA 99).

Background

During payroll processing for period 05/2026, wage type 1419 was configured with:

  • Processing Class 20 = 4 (incorrect)

After investigation, we found that the correct configuration should be:

  • Processing Class 20 = 3

The correction was transported and applied successfully.

Current Situation

  • Payroll for 06/2026 has been recalculated after the correction.
  • Posting to FI for 06/2026 works successfully.
  • However, posting to FI for 05/2026 still fails with error:

Message No. 3G103 - Accounting balance is not zero

The payroll results for May were generated before the correction of Processing Class 20.

Constraints

  • Payroll area is already closed for 05/2026.
  • We would like to avoid reopening and recalculating the entire May payroll if possible.
  • June payroll already contains the corrected configuration and FI posting works without issues.

Questions

  1. Is there any standard SAP method to regenerate posting results for period 05/2026 without a complete payroll recalculation?
  2. Can posting documents be rebuilt after a Processing Class 20 correction?
  3. Has anyone encountered a similar issue where a Processing Class change caused posting imbalance in a closed payroll period?
  4. What would be the recommended SAP best practice in this scenario?

Any guidance or experience would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

SAP HCM Payroll (International Payroll - MOLGA 99)

entry.title: Re: Unable to reverse UD and stock reversal for incoming...
entry.content:
Dear @newaskarvinay,

The Usage Decision can be reset in the Manage Usage Decisions app but this does not automatically reverse the stock posting that already moved the material to unrestricted-use stock. When the Usage Decision is reset, SAP initializes the UD code and quality score and changes the inspection lot status from UD to UDRE. However, SAP explicitly confirms that the related stock postings are not revoked and the existing material documents remain unchanged. This is standard system behavior in Public Cloud

More details below:

SAP Help Manage Usage Decisions

SAP KBA 3359470 - The "Reset Usage Decision" function in app Manage Usage Decisions cannot reverse the stock postings of the inspection lot

Therefore, if the material was mistakenly approved and moved to unrestricted stock the correction has to be done in two steps:

  1. Reset the Usage Decision in Manage Usage Decisions
  2. Post the stock back from unrestricted-use stock to quality inspection stock, for example via Transfer Stock In-Plant using movement type 322. SAP documents movement type 322 as the reversal movement from unrestricted-use stock back to stock in quality inspection

More details below:

SAP Help Transfer Stock - In-Plant

After the stock is reposted to quality inspection the material can be handled again in the newly generated inspection lot, according to the required quality process

If the business requirement is to reverse the original goods receipt after the Usage Decision has been recorded this is not possible as a direct GR reversal in Public Cloud. SAP confirms that this is working as designed. In such a case the correction should be handled through the return-to-vendor process, using a return purchase order and if relevant a supplier credit memo

More details below:

SAP KBA 3755098 - Cannot reverse goods receipt after usage decision is recorded - SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Recommendation for configuration role based on standa...
entry.content:
Hello @belen_fernandez2,

I can not give you specific answers to the two questions regarding SAP recommendations. However, I can give you my personal experiences of using a SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT "copy" since two years.

We are using the business role based on the business role template SAP_BR_BPC_EXPERT for our internal SAP Application Management to handle configuration access.

We are in fact using about 700 apps. So in the derived business role we deactivated some apps that we do not need.

➡️  The message is only a remark, not a full stop. So technically, you will be able to work with the business role.

Performance issues? Yes, those do appear during role adjustment and transport of business role.

  • If the business role activation process gets stuck during role adjustment, I use the return button to get back to the “Maintain Business Roles” overview (draft will be saved, then open the business role again – that usually helps.
  • And for the transport: a transport containing this specific business role will take quite some time. So, I usually perform it at the end of business hours.

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Kerisha

 

Kerisha Lawrence

Public Cloud | IAM, Procurement, Post Go-Live

https://www.linkedin.com/in/kerisha-lawrence

entry.title: Re: Start Small. Think at Scale. Business Professionals Can Use SAP & Claude to Build a Business

It answers, 'why this, why now, and what do we get for it.' Once that foundation is solid, the real work begins defining the problem precisely, measuring the current state, identifying root causes, and only then designing the solution at scale. SAP and Claude together accelerate that journey, but the discipline of validating each step before scaling with the right sponsor and stakeholders engaged throughout is what separates sustainable adoption from costly trial and error. Appreciate your thoughtful engagement!

entry.title: Migrating from Microsoft AutoGen to Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF) with SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Introduction

As part of the Intelligent Product Recommendation (IPR) team at SAP, we have been using Microsoft AutoGen to implement agentic workflows — specifically the reflection pattern — on top of SAP AI Core. If you have read the earlier post Microsoft AutoGen v0.6.1 with SAP AI Core, you already know how we integrated AutoGen using AzureOpenAIChatCompletionClient and the gen_ai_hub_sdk to obtain tokens and deployment URLs.

Microsoft has now released the Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF). MAF is designed and maintained by the same teams and combines AutoGen's simple multi-agent abstractions with Semantic Kernel's enterprise features: session-based state management, type safety, middleware, telemetry, and graph-based workflows. In short, MAF is the next generation of AutoGen.

This post documents exactly what changed when we migrated our production codebase from AutoGen to MAF, and why the SAP community building agentic solutions on AI Core using AutoGen should consider making the same move.

Why Migrate from AutoGen to MAF?

Here is a quick comparison of the key differences:

AutoGen

Microsoft Agent Framework (MAF)

autogen_agentchat, autogen_core packages

agent_framework, agent_framework.orchestrations packages

AssistantAgent with model_client=, system_message=

Agent with client=, instructions=

RoundRobinGroupChat for multi-agent

GroupChatBuilder with pluggable selection and termination

Manual tool wrapper classes + manual schema registration

Plain @tool-decorated functions, schema auto-generated from type hints

No built-in context window management

ContextWindowCompactionStrategy, SlidingWindowStrategy

No session state across turns

AgentSession for multi-turn conversation state

No middleware layer

AgentMiddleware for logging, retry, rate-limiting

autogen_core.models types (UserMessage, AssistantMessage ...)

Plain dicts / ChatResponse from agent_framework

Framework-Level Concept Mapping

Before looking at code, here is the one-to-one concept mapping:

AutoGen Concept

MAF Equivalent

AssistantAgent

Agent

model_client=

client=

system_message=

instructions=

RoundRobinGroupChat

GroupChatBuilder

TextMentionTermination

Custom termination predicate (lambda / function)

FunctionTool + wrapper class

@tool decorator on a plain async function

TaskResult.messages[-1].content

AgentResponse.text

team.run(task=question)

workflow.run(question)

autogen_core.models.UserMessage

dict with role/content keys

ModelInfo(supports_functions=True)

dict {"supports_functions": True}

Key Code Changes

1. Agent Constructor

The AssistantAgent constructor is replaced by Agent with renamed parameters:

Before (AutoGen):

from autogen_agentchat.agents import AssistantAgent
 
weather_agent = AssistantAgent(
    name="weather_agent",
    description="Fetches weather information",
    model_client=model_client,
    tools=[get_weather],
    system_message="You are a weather assistant."
)

After (MAF):

from agent_framework import Agent
 
weather_agent = Agent(
    client=model_client,               # model_client= -> client=
    name="weather_agent",
    instructions="You are a weather assistant.",  # system_message= -> instructions=
    tools=[get_weather]                # description= dropped (not supported in MAF)
)

2. Tool Definition — Wrapper Class to @tool Decorator

AutoGen required a wrapper class that held state and manually registered the JSON schema on the model client. MAF replaces this with a plain @tool-decorated async function — no wrapper class, no manual schema registration.

Before (AutoGen):

from autogen_agentchat.tools import FunctionTool
 
class WeatherToolWrapper:
    def __init__(self, units: str = "metric"):
        self.units = units
 
    async def get_weather(self, city: str) -> str:
        return f"The weather in {city} is 73 degrees and Sunny ({self.units})."
 
weather_wrapper = WeatherToolWrapper(units="imperial")
tools = [FunctionTool(weather_wrapper.get_weather, description="Get the weather for a city.")]
self._register_tool_on_client(weather_wrapper)   # manual schema mutation

After (MAF):

from agent_framework import tool
 
@tool
async def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Get the current weather for a given city."""
    return f"The weather in {city} is 73 degrees and Sunny."
 
tools = [get_weather]
# No wrapper class needed. Schema is auto-generated from type hints and docstring.

3. Multi-Agent Group Chat — RoundRobinGroupChat to GroupChatBuilder

The RoundRobinGroupChat with TextMentionTermination is replaced by GroupChatBuilder, which accepts explicit selection and termination functions.

Before (AutoGen):

from autogen_agentchat.agents import AssistantAgent
from autogen_agentchat.teams import RoundRobinGroupChat
from autogen_agentchat.conditions import TextMentionTermination
 
weather_agent = AssistantAgent(
    name="weather_agent", model_client=model_client,
    tools=[get_weather],
    system_message="You are a weather assistant. Fetch weather data using the tool."
)
reviewer_agent = AssistantAgent(
    name="reviewer_agent", model_client=model_client,
    system_message="Review the weather response. If complete, reply DONE."
)
 
team = RoundRobinGroupChat(
    [weather_agent, reviewer_agent],
    max_turns=4,
    termination_condition=TextMentionTermination("DONE")
)
result = await team.run(task="What is the weather in New York and London?")
final_text = result.messages[-1].content

After (MAF):

from agent_framework import Agent
from agent_framework_orchestrations import GroupChatBuilder, GroupChatState
from agent_framework import AgentResponse
 
weather_agent = Agent(
    client=model_client, name="weather_agent",
    instructions="You are a weather assistant. Fetch weather data using the tool.",
    tools=[get_weather]
)
reviewer_agent = Agent(
    client=model_client, name="reviewer_agent",
    instructions="Review the weather response. If complete, reply DONE."
)
 
def _round_robin_selection(state: GroupChatState) -> str:
    names = list(state.participants.keys())
    return names[state.current_round % len(names)]
 
def _review_approved(messages) -> bool:
    return any(
        "DONE" in m.text
        for m in messages
        if getattr(m, "author_name", "") == "reviewer_agent"
    )
 
workflow = GroupChatBuilder(
    participants=[weather_agent, reviewer_agent],
    selection_func=_round_robin_selection,
    termination_condition=_review_approved,
    max_rounds=4,
    intermediate_output_from=[weather_agent],
).build()
 
run_result = await workflow.run("What is the weather in New York and London?")
 
final_text = ""
for event in run_result:
    if event.type == "intermediate":
        response = event.data
        if isinstance(response, AgentResponse):
            candidate = " ".join(m.text for m in response.messages if m.text).strip()
            if candidate:
                final_text = candidate

4. Single-Agent run() Call

Before (AutoGen):

response = await weather_agent.run(task="What is the weather in Paris?")
return response.messages[-1].content

After (MAF):

response = await weather_agent.run("What is the weather in Paris?")
return response.text

5. Custom LLM Client Adapter (SAP AI Core Integration)

Because we use a custom AICoreLLMClient (backed by gen_ai_hub_sdk) instead of a direct Azure OpenAI client, the adapter had to be updated. AutoGen expected a create() method returning a CreateResult object. MAF expects a get_response() method returning a ChatResponse.

Before (AutoGen):

async def create(self, messages, tools=None, **kwargs) -> CreateResult:
    resp = await asyncio.to_thread(self.generate_response, messages)
    return self._build_create_result(resp)
 
def capabilities(self) -> ModelInfo:
    return ModelInfo(supports_functions=True, supports_vision=self.support_images)

After (MAF):

async def get_response(self, messages, *, stream=False, options=None, ...) -> ChatResponse:
    from agent_framework import ChatResponse, Message
    # extract instructions and tools from options, then run tool-call loop
    for iteration in range(max_tool_iterations):
        llm_resp = await asyncio.to_thread(self.generate_response, loop_messages)
        if not llm_resp.has_tool_calls:
            return ChatResponse(messages=[Message("assistant", [content_text])], ...)
        # execute tools, append results, continue loop
    ...
 
def capabilities(self) -> dict:
    return {"supports_functions": True, "supports_vision": self.support_images}

6. Dependency Changes

Update your requirements.txt or pyproject.toml as follows.

Remove: 

autogen-core
autogen-agentchat

Add:

agent-framework-core

Future Works — MAF Features that could be used to enhance Agentic Systems

MAF (agent_framework==1.9.0) ships with several capabilities we have identified as high-value but have not yet wired into the codebase. Here is the prioritised roadmap:

Feature 1 — create_harness_agent (Low Risk, High Value)

Replace the bare Agent(...) constructor with create_harness_agent(). This single call automatically enables two-phase context window compaction and per-LLM-call history persistence with zero behavioural change to the existing API.

Feature 2 — AgentSession for Multi-Turn State (Low Risk)

AgentSession maintains conversation history across multiple run() calls on the same agent. Without it, every run() is stateless — the agent has no memory of previous turns. With AgentSession, prior context is preserved across calls within the same request.

Feature 3 — TodoProvider for Partial-Result Recovery (Medium Risk)

TodoProvider gives the agent five built-in tools (todos_add, todos_complete, todos_get_remaining, etc.) to checkpoint progress within a long task. If the request times out or the process restarts, a resumed call continues from the last open todo rather than starting from scratch.

Feature 4 — BackgroundAgentsProvider for Parallel Sub-Tasks (Medium-High Risk)

BackgroundAgentsProvider lets a parent orchestrator agent dispatch multiple sub-tasks to background sub-agents in parallel (non-blocking). For example, a coordinator agent could fetch weather for 10 cities simultaneously rather than sequentially — reducing total latency from 10x to roughly 1x (limited by the slowest sub-task).

Feature 5 — AgentMiddleware for Cross-Cutting Concerns (Medium Risk)

AgentMiddleware intercepts every agent.run() call, enabling request/response logging, automatic retry on failure, and token budget enforcement — without any changes to the agent code itself.

Feature 6 — LocalEvaluator for CI/CD Quality Gates (Low Risk)

LocalEvaluator and evaluate_agent provide a built-in evaluation framework. Adding a keyword_check("degrees") check ensures every weather response contains temperature data; tool_called_check("get_weather") verifies the agent always calls the tool instead of hallucinating. These can be added to the existing test suite as a CI gate with no changes to production code.

Conclusion

Migrating from AutoGen to MAF required targeted changes to three core areas:

  • Agent construction — AssistantAgent replaced by Agent with renamed parameters
  • Tool registration — wrapper class and manual schema replaced by @tool decorator
  • Multi-agent orchestration — RoundRobinGroupChat replaced by GroupChatBuilder

The migration also gave us a cleaner LLM client adapter (get_response instead of create), removed all AutoGen type dependencies from message handling, and set the codebase up for the richer MAF features described in the Future Works section above.

If your team is already using AutoGen on SAP AI Core, the migration is straightforward — the concept mapping is one-to-one and the packages are drop-in replacements. MAF gives you a production-grade foundation (context management, middleware, evaluation) that AutoGen simply did not have.

Elevate with AI !!

References:

Microsoft Agentic Framework: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework/overview/?pivots=programming-language-python

Migration Guide: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework/migration-guide/from-autogen/

entry.title: Asset Accoutning is going live in 2026, however there are some assets which were purchased in 2025.
entry.content:
Asset accounting set up is done in 2026 and is going live in Jun 2026.  There are some assets purchased in 2025 and want them to capitalize in 2026 however the depreciation start date would be 2025. I have proposed Asset migration procedure.  The Assets should be created by using AS91 Tcode and post the APC and Accumulated depreciation till Dec 2025 via Take over values option and post a manual journal entry.  Below is the proposed entry :

Asst Account Dr

Accumulated Depreciation Account Cr

Asset Migration account Cr.

After posting the above entry the balance which is laying in Asset Migration account, I have proposed to move asset migration account balances to retained earnings account.

However, client is asking a different approach.  Client is saying instead of the above process.  Capitalize the assets using AS01, the capitalization date should be 23rd April 2025 and depreciation start should be 23rd Aprils 2025 and post the entire depreciation from April 2025 to May 2026 in Jun 2026 depreciation run.  Is this approach doable?  If yes, how?

entry.title: Re: How to hide the custom form uibb in standard OVP FPM...
entry.content:
Sharing the solution. Hope will be helpful to other colleagues.

Step 1:  Find the tabbed UIBB under which you created a new form/list uibb.

Step 2: Select it, below it will be selected in the UIBB list.

Step 3: You already created a custom configuration and wired it. Find the connector tab and select it.

Step 4: Inside the connector tab, click on Enhance.

Step 5: Add your new custom tab and check the field "Hide If No Lead Sel".

  • we are uploading two payment medium files into the system
  • each upload generates an incoming payment order and an outgoing payment order which are displayed in "Manage Payments" app

In this customizing step, clicking on the Rule ID matchcode, different rules are displayed, but I cannot find the step in which they are maintained:

I would appreciate your help.

Thank you.

entry.title: Substitution rule reference bkpf-xref by PO number
entry.content:
Dear all,

We are going to do self invoicing for PO with periodic invoicing plan using tile create invoicing plan settlement (MRIS). It is working fine, however to allow our supplier to know what was paid we would like to fill the PO number in the field BKPF-XREF for the accounting document of the MM invoices.

I created a substitution rule on header level with prerequisite tcode = MRIS with a user exit. The rule is correctly triggered how the reference is not fed. Here is the user exit

"

form u301.
  data(lv_mm_invoice) = conv belnr_d( bkpf-xblnr ).

  if lv_mm_invoice is initial.
    return.
  endif.

  select distinct ekpo~ebeln,
                  fplt~afdat,
                  fplt~fkdat
    from fplt
           inner join
             ekpo on  ekpo~fplnr = fplt~fplnr
                  and ekpo~loekz = @Space
               inner join
                 rseg on  rseg~ebeln = ekpo~ebeln
                      and rseg~belnr = @LV_mm_invoice
    where fplt~afdat <= @SY-DATUM
      and fplt~fksaf  = @c_inv_plan_item_status-open
    into table @DATA(lt_po_invoicing_dates).

  if sy-subrc <> 0.
    return.
  endif.

"

It is not working because  bkpf-xref is not filled with the MM document directly but with value "*MM". So is there please another way to get the PO number ? Or to get the MM document number to get the PO ?

Do you please have any other suggestion on how to fill the PO number in bkpf-xref ? Knowing that according sap documentation rules on complete document don't work with fiori

Best regards

entry.title: 🇺🇸 ABAP AI Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers (York, United States)
entry.content:
In this CodeJam you will learn how to use ABAP capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers, including predictive code completion, unit test generation for ABAP CDS and ABAP code, and code explanation.

Speed up your development with ABAP Cloud using SAP Joule's AI-assisted features: predictive code completion that understands ABAP Cloud semantics, automatic unit test generation for CDS views and ABAP classes, and natural language code explanation to understand unfamiliar code quickly.

Location and Date:

This is a free in-person event (not virtual) and is planned for Thursday, August 13th, 2026 from 10:00 - 16:00 EST in York, Pennsylvania, United States. The language of the event and the content will be English.

Event Address: The Yorktowne Hotel, 48 East Market Street, York, Pennsylvania, 17401, United States

The instructor is @RichHeilman.

The co-instructor is @thomas_jung.

The event is kindly hosted by SAP. Thank you!

Prerequisites:

Install ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse on your local machine before the event: https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/abap-install-adt.html

Please note that spaces are limited! We can only accommodate 30 attendees. If you want to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. A confirmation email will be sent to all registered participants 1 week before the event.

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: Re: Submitting XML via Mail with SAP DRC and SAP ERP
entry.content:
Hello Maurice,

Yes, indeed—within the SAP EDOC_COCKPIT, you can configure two communication channels: one via Peppol and the other via Email. Both variants leverage SAP DRC for the transmission of electronic documents.

Using the customizing tables SM30 → EDOEUBUPADOCTYPV and EDOEUBUPAV, you can define which communication channel and content type is assigned to each customer.

Since you already have the correct reference for setting up the SAP DRC email scenario, the next step is to activate and configure the required connections and parameters as described in the official documentation.

However, before proceeding with the implementation, it is strongly recommended to first apply all relevant SAP Notes for the application components:

  • CA-GTF-CSC-EDO-DE
  • CA-GTF-CSC-EDO-PAP

I hope this helps you move forward with your implementation.

Best Regards, Günter

entry.title: Re: TM Public Cloud - transportation scheduling without 46P
entry.content:
Good day @joannab 

Thank you for your question.

Your understanding is partly correct: you do not require 46P to create or manually schedule a freight order. However, it is required to activate the standard integration with SAP HANA spatial services for automatic geocoding and automatic distance and duration determination.

See Managing Locations and Geographical Information for more insights.

The planned dates can still appear because departure and arrival dates do not necessarily prove that SAP has calculated a road distance.

Scheduling determines the start and end times for freight order activities, including departure, arrival, loading, and unloading. These dates can be influenced by the time windows received from the freight unit or preceding busines document.

In the Starter System, the dates may therefore originate from the following:

  • Pickup and delivery time windows transferred to the freight unit
  • Dates already available in the sales order or delivery
  • Manually maintained freight order dates
  • Distance and duration values previously stored in the distance and duration buffer

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/199883529a4a7b6ae10000000a423f68.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/513060d226c44f299c634fd4b727e546.html?locale=en-US

An empty distance field in the location does not confirm that no distance or duration exists. Distance and duration are determined for a pair of locations and can be stored in the buffer rather than directly in the individual location master.

Without the SAP HANA spatial services integration, the standard options you have are:

  1. Manually maintain or adjust the planned dates and time in the freight order.
  2. Use Pickup and delivery time windows inherited from the originating sales order, delivery, or freight unit.
  3. Receive planned dates, distances, or durations from an external transportation planning system through the released freight order integration.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/31058013fa80465bb6587cf9dba4f861.html?locale=en-US

I recommend you open Manage Freight Orders and compare the freight order stage duration with the freight unit pickup and delivery time windows.

Also check whether the stage contains a distance value. See Manage Freight Orders on how the app automatically calculates distance and duration.

 

Let me know if you have further question.

Best regards

Chris
